does: This paper introduces an iterative data generation pipeline to detect and control sycophancy in language models through activation steering. Rather than relying on simple binary contrastive pairs, the method generates cascading samples that exhibit graded levels of a target behavior, enabling identification of linear features whose activation strength scales with sycophancy. The authors demonstrate that sycophancy features discovered through this approach form linearly separable subspaces in model activations, allowing for more precise selection of steering targets than baseline methods. The work shows that this cascade-based approach yields clearer correspondence between identified model activations and the undesired sycophant behavior, improving both detection accuracy and steering effectiveness.
connects: This work builds on mechanistic interpretability and activation steering literature that emerged from research on understanding model internals through linear probes and directional steering methods. It extends prior work on behavioral control through activation interventions, particularly methods that use contrastive samples to isolate behavioral features. The paper responds to limitations in simple binary-pair approaches for feature isolation, drawing on insights from geometric analysis of model representations. The focus on sycophancy connects to growing empirical work on alignment failures and systematic model biases, while the cascading data generation approach relates to curriculum learning and progressive feature discovery in deep neural networks.
enables: This work enables more targeted steering of model behaviors by providing a scalable pipeline for feature discovery that could extend beyond sycophancy to other unwanted behaviors. The method of using cascading samples to isolate linearly disentangled features could support more reliable control techniques in deployed models. The approach points toward better interpretability tools for red-teaming and behavioral correction without full retraining. Future work could combine this with other alignment techniques, apply the cascade method to other behavioral phenomena in LLMs, and integrate findings into automated safeguards. The linear separability results suggest potential for developing lightweight steering interventions that scale across different model sizes and architectures.
